skill.name: obsidian-markdown
skill.description: Create and edit Obsidian Flavored Markdown with wikilinks, embeds, callouts, properties, and other Obsidian-specific syntax. Use when working with .md files in Obsidian, or when the user mentions wikilinks, callouts, frontmatter, tags, embeds, or Obsidian notes.
skill.summary: Master Obsidian Flavored Markdown syntax including wikilinks, embeds, callouts, properties, and tags for creating well-structured notes.
skill.author: kepano

security_audit.summary: All 121 static findings for 'external_commands' are false positives from Markdown code fences in documentation files. The detected backticks are part of Markdown syntax (```) for code blocks, not shell execution. The network findings are legitimate documentation URLs to Obsidian help pages. This is a documentation-only skill with no executable code, making it safe for publication.

content.value_statement: Standard Markdown lacks features for linking notes and organizing knowledge. This skill helps you create properly formatted Obsidian notes with wikilinks, embeds, callouts, and properties.
actual_capabilities: Create wikilinks for internal note connections with heading and block references
Embed content from other notes, images, PDFs, and media files using Obsidian syntax
Format callouts with 12+ types including note, warning, tip, question, and custom CSS callouts
Write YAML frontmatter properties for metadata including tags, aliases, dates, and custom fields
Use Obsidian-specific features like highlighted text, comments, and nested tags
Link Mermaid diagram nodes to Obsidian notes using internal-link class
limitations: Does not execute Obsidian commands or interact with the Obsidian application
Cannot verify if linked notes exist in your vault or validate block IDs
Standard Markdown features (headings, lists, tables) are assumed knowledge
Cannot test or render Mermaid diagrams, only provide the syntax

use_cases.title: Knowledge Base Creator
use_cases.description: Build a connected knowledge base using wikilinks to create a graph of related notes. Use properties for metadata and callouts to highlight key information across your documentation.
use_cases.target_user: Researchers and academics organizing complex information

use_cases.title: Project Note Manager
use_cases.description: Create structured project notes with frontmatter properties for status, dates, and tags. Use callouts to track deadlines and embed related documents for quick reference.

use_cases.title: Technical Documentation Writer
use_cases.description: Write technical docs with proper Obsidian formatting including code blocks, diagrams, and cross-references. Use block links to connect specific sections across documentation files.
use_cases.target_user: Developers creating API documentation or guides

prompt_templates.title: Create a Basic Note
prompt_templates.prompt: Create an Obsidian note about '{topic}'. Include frontmatter with title, tags, and date. Add 2-3 wikilinks to related concepts. Use a callout to highlight key information.
prompt_templates.scenario: Starting a new note with proper Obsidian structure

prompt_templates.title: Link Between Notes
prompt_templates.prompt: I'm working on notes about '{topic}'. Show me how to create wikilinks to connect '{note_a}' and '{note_b}'. Include examples of heading links and block references.
prompt_templates.scenario: Connecting related notes in a knowledge base

prompt_templates.title: Format Complex Callout
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a nested callout structure for '{use_case}'. Use an outer callout of type '{outer_type}' and an inner callout of type '{inner_type}'. Make the outer callout collapsible.

prompt_templates.title: Add Rich Properties
prompt_templates.prompt: Design YAML frontmatter for a '{document_type}' note. Include properties for: tags, aliases, cssclasses, status, date, and 2-3 custom fields relevant to this type of document.
prompt_templates.scenario: Adding structured metadata to notes for filtering and search

output_examples.input: Create a project note with frontmatter, a warning callout, and wikilinks
output_examples.output:
---
title: Website Redesign
tags:
  - project
  - active
status: in-progress
due: 2024-03-01
aliases:
  - Site Redesign
  - 2024 Web Project
---

# Website Redesign

> [!warning] Budget Constraint
> Keep total costs under $5000 for design assets.

This project updates [[Company Website]] with new branding.

## Tasks
- [ ] Review [[Design System]]
- [ ] Update [[Navigation Structure]]

output_examples.input: Show me how to embed an image and link to a specific section
output_examples.output:

![[architecture-diagram.png|800]]

output_examples.input: Create a research note with nested callouts and block references
output_examples.output:
---
title: Machine Learning Research
tags:
  - research
  - ml
---

# ML Model Comparison

> [!tip] Key Finding
> > [!note] Performance Data
> > Transformer models achieved 94% accuracy compared to 78% for CNN approaches.

## Results

The best model uses attention mechanisms. ^best-model

See [[Related Research#Hyperparameters]] for optimization details.

best_practices: Use wikilinks [[Like This]] for internal notes and standard Markdown [links](url) for external websites
Define block IDs with ^identifier for paragraphs, or place them on a separate line after lists and quotes
Organize tags hierarchically with slashes (e.g., #project/active) for better filtering in the graph view
Use the callout types that match your intent: note for neutral info, tip for suggestions, warning for cautions, and danger for critical issues
anti_patterns: Do not use backticks in property values or frontmatter as they may break YAML parsing
Avoid creating circular wikilink references where Note A links to Note B and Note B links back to Note A without clear context
Do not mix Markdown links [text](url) and wikilinks [[text]] inconsistently - choose the right syntax for each use case
Never use special characters in tags that require quotes or escaping, as this breaks tag functionality

faq.question: What is the difference between wikilinks and Markdown links?
faq.answer: Use wikilinks [[Note Name]] for internal notes in your vault. Obsidian tracks these links automatically when you rename files. Use standard Markdown links [text](url) for external websites and resources outside your vault.

faq.question: How do I create a collapsible callout?
faq.answer: Add a hyphen - after the callout type to make it collapsed by default, or a plus + to make it expanded by default. Example: > [!faq]- Collapsed content or > [!faq]+ Expanded content.

faq.question: Can I nest callouts within each other?
faq.answer: Yes, nest callouts by adding extra > symbols before the nested callout. Example: > [!question] Outer > > [!note] Inner note. You can nest multiple levels deep for complex information organization.

faq.question: What is the difference between tags in frontmatter versus inline tags?
faq.answer: Both work the same way. Frontmatter tags use YAML list format with - for each tag. Inline tags use # anywhere in your note content. Nested tags use slashes like #project/active. Choose the format that fits your workflow.

faq.question: How do I link to a specific part of another note?
faq.answer: Use heading links with # or block links with ^. Examples: [[Note Name#Heading Name]] for sections, or [[Note Name#^block-id]] for specific paragraphs. Define block IDs by adding ^identifier to any content.

faq.question: What are the most useful frontmatter properties?
faq.answer: The default properties are tags for searchability, aliases for alternative names in link suggestions, and cssclasses for custom styling. You can add any custom properties like status, date, rating, or due dates for filtering and querying.
